all.critique: The backward pass is applied to the combined loss computed from all components, yet the update is intended to affect only one parameter group at a time. Without explicitly isolating the gradients (for example, by disabling gradient flow or detaching branches for the inactive group), parameters not meant to be updated may inadvertently receive gradient updates. This contradicts the intended alternating update mechanism.

Only the selected optimizer’s gradients are zeroed out in each iteration. This may lead to gradient accumulation in the non-selected parameter group over iterations, which could cause unexpected behavior when that optimizer is later activated.

The analysis refers to methods such as get_eigen_params(), get_feature_params(), update_eigenbasis(), update_features(), and compute_loss() without specifying their implementations or expected signatures. This vagueness may lead to undefined behavior or confusion when trying to align the training loop with the paper’s methodology.

With tau1 set to 0 (and tau2 > 0), the condition (t % (tau1 + tau2)) < tau1 is never met. As a consequence, the synthetic eigenbasis parameters are never updated, which may conflict with the original paper’s intent if alternating updates were required for both parameter sets.

The procedure for computing and logging the total variation (TV) metric is vaguely defined. More clarity is needed on how the synthetic graph’s TV is calculated (e.g., the detailed formulation using the synthetic Laplacian or adjacency matrix) and how it is monitored relative to the real graph.

The analysis assumes an epoch-level training loop without discussing the possibility of mini-batch updates. Clarification on whether the updates should occur per epoch or per batch is needed for a complete and reproducible implementation.
